flashcards.front: What is a one-dimensional array in C?
flashcards.back: A one-dimensional array is a collection of elements of the same data type stored in contiguous memory locations, accessed using a single index. It provides a way to store multiple related values under one variable name.

flashcards.front: What is the syntax to declare a one-dimensional array in C?
flashcards.back: The syntax is: data_type array_name[size]; For example: int marks[10]; declares an array of 10 integers named 'marks'.

flashcards.front: What is zero-based indexing in arrays?
flashcards.back: In C, array indices start from 0, not 1. The first element is accessed using index 0, second with index 1, and so on. If an array has n elements, valid indices are 0 to n-1.

flashcards.front: How do you initialize a one-dimensional array during declaration?
flashcards.back: Two methods: 1) Specify values: int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}; 2) Partial initialization: int arr[5] = {10, 20}; (remaining elements are 0). If size omitted, compiler counts elements: int arr[] = {1,2,3};

flashcards.front: What is the difference between int arr[5] and int arr[]?
flashcards.back: int arr[5] allocates fixed space for 5 elements regardless of initial values. int arr[] automatically sizes the array based on the number of initialization values provided.

flashcards.front: What is array bounds checking in C?
flashcards.back: C does not perform automatic bounds checking. Accessing arr[-1] or arr[n] (where n is array size) results in undefined behavior. Programmers must ensure indices are within 0 to n-1.

flashcards.front: How is memory allocated for a one-dimensional array?
flashcards.back: Arrays allocate contiguous memory locations. For int arr[5] on a system with 4-byte ints, if base address is 1000, memory locations are: arr[0]=1000, arr[1]=1004, arr[2]=1008, arr[3]=1012, arr[4]=1016.

flashcards.front: How do you pass a one-dimensional array to a function?
flashcards.back: Pass array name (which decays to pointer): void func(int arr[], int n) or void func(int *arr, int n). The function receives the base address, so modifications affect the original array.

flashcards.front: What is the formula to calculate sum of all elements in an array?
flashcards.back: Initialize sum = 0, then iterate through each element: for(i=0; i<n; i++) sum += arr[i]; The sum can then compute average as sum/n.

flashcards.front: How do you find the maximum element in a one-dimensional array?
flashcards.back: Initialize max = arr[0], then compare each element: for(i=1; i<n; i++) if(arr[i] > max) max = arr[i]; This requires O(n) time complexity.

flashcards.front: What is linear search in a one-dimensional array?
flashcards.back: Linear search checks each element sequentially from the first until target is found or array ends. Worst case: O(n) comparisons. Best case: O(1) if element is at first position.

flashcards.front: What is binary search and can it be applied to all one-dimensional arrays?
flashcards.back: Binary search divides search space in half each step, requiring O(log n) time. It ONLY works on SORTED arrays. For unsorted arrays, elements must be sorted first or linear search must be used.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between static and dynamic array allocation in C?
flashcards.back: Static: int arr[10]; - size fixed at compile time, stack memory. Dynamic: int *arr = (int*)malloc(n*sizeof(int)); - size determined at runtime, heap memory, must free() after use.

flashcards.front: What is array reversal and how is it performed?
flashcards.back: Array reversal swaps elements from beginning and end moving toward center: for(i=0, j=n-1; i<j; i++, j--) { temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp; } Time complexity: O(n).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between arr[i] and &arr[i]?
flashcards.back: arr[i] gives the value at index i. &arr[i] gives the memory address (pointer) of that element. arr (without index) also gives base address of the array.
